summary:
The trade in cedar timber from the forests of Lebanon and the broader Levantine coast was one of the most economically significant commercial relationships of the ancient Near East, active from at least 2900 BC through the first millennium BC. Both Egypt, which lacked suitable large timber for construction and shipbuilding, and Mesopotamia, situated in a largely treeless river plain, depended on Levantine cedar and other high-quality timber for monumental building, naval construction, and elite prestige goods. The port city of Byblos (Gebel) on the Lebanese coast was the primary hub of this trade.

Egyptian records document cedar imports from the early Old Kingdom onward. The wood was used for the construction of royal funerary barges, coffins, temple doors, furniture, and the large wooden components of pyramid temples. The Egyptian name for Byblos — 'Keben' — became synonymous with the imported timber itself. Ships carrying cedar logs from Byblos to Egypt are depicted in Old Kingdom reliefs, and archaeological evidence confirms the use of Lebanese cedar in royal tomb construction as early as the First Dynasty.

For Mesopotamia, cedar timber — glorified in the [[event:1728|Epic of Gilgamesh]] as the prized resource of the Cedar Forest guarded by the monster Humbaba — held both practical and mythological significance. The export of cedar from Lebanon thus connected the civilisations of the ancient Near East to the Levantine coast in a relationship of mutual economic dependence, and the city of Byblos thrived as a commercial intermediary whose prosperity rested on its control of this essential resource.
